aliases: Lan Ting Xu Beef Casserole Set

english: Lan Ting Xu Beef Casserole Set

region_of_origin: China (Lanzhou / Northwest China tradition), served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Braised beef dishes; Chinese set meals with rice or noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Chinese braised beef casserole set meal. FOODON_03302757 (beef stew) is the closest defensible parent category covering slow-cooked beef in liquid with vegetables.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Beef meat dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Chinese braised beef casserole set meal in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific composite Chinese beef casserole set meal.

serving.description: 1 set meal plate (beef casserole with rice or noodles and side vegetables)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving consists of approximately 550 g, comprising roughly 200 g braised beef with sauce, 200 g steamed rice or noodles, and 150 g side vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, braised, composite dish — adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Beef, chuck, arm pot roast, braised (NDB 13316) combined with cooked white rice and vegetables, weighted by typical set meal composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chinese beef casserole set meal exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for braised beef chuck (NDB 13316), cooked white rice (NDB 20045), and cooked bok choy (NDB 11740) in approximate proportions of 36% beef with sauce, 36% rice, and 28% vegetables, then normalised to 100 g of the composite set meal. Sauce contribution (soy sauce, sugar, oil) is factored into the estimate. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (GI ~64) and mixed beef-rice composite estimated at GI 54 using weighted carbohydrate contribution method
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) — American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese beef casserole set meal. GI estimated at 54 using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method: white rice (GI 64, ~36% of serving carbohydrate contribution) combined with braised beef component (GI ~0 for protein-fat dominant component) and vegetable component (GI ~15). Weighted composite GI calculated as approximately 54, placing it in the low category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 96.25) / 100 ≈ 52.0 g, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 17.5 × 5.5 = 96.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single 550 g set meal serving provides approximately 2090 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by white rice component; moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the white rice component contributes a low GI of approximately 54 and a glycemic load of ~52 g per set meal serving; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion can meaningfully lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that this dish provides moderate saturated fat (~8.3 g per serving) and high sodium (~2090 mg per serving); choosing leaner beef cuts and requesting less sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Casserole Set Meal (红烧牛肉套餐) is a Chinese braised beef dish served as a complete set meal, typically comprising slow-cooked beef in a rich soy-based sauce accompanied by steamed white rice or noodles and a side of blanched green vegetables such as bok choy. The beef, commonly chuck or brisket, is braised for an extended period with soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, ginger, garlic, star anise, five-spice powder, and rock sugar, producing a deeply savoury, slightly sweet, and aromatic sauce with tender, fall-apart meat. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Chinese noodle restaurants, including Lanzhou-style establishments such as Lan Ting Xu, where it may be paired with hand-pulled noodles rather than rice. The set meal format makes it a filling, protein-rich main course. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~9.8 g/100 g) from the beef, moderate carbohydrate (~17.5 g/100 g) from the rice or noodle component, and moderate fat (~4.2 g/100 g). Sodium content is elevated due to the soy-based braising liquid, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the composite meal is estimated at 54 (low), driven primarily by the white rice component. The dish is dairy-free but contains gluten from soy sauce and noodles.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chinese beef casserole set meal was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for braised beef chuck, cooked white rice, and cooked bok choy in proportions representative of a typical set meal. GI is estimated using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method anchored to University of Sydney GI Database values for white rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy components (braised beef chuck NDB 13316, cooked white rice NDB 20045, cooked bok choy NDB 11740) weighted at approximately 36% beef with sauce, 36% rice, and 28% vegetables, normalised to 100 g composite. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this estimation. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this estimation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the component source entries. Sodium estimate of 380 mg/100 g accounts for soy sauce contribution in the braising liquid; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. Halal status is marked false as beef sourcing and preparation at the specific restaurant (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles) has not been verified against a certified halal authority. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (54 × 96.25) / 100 ≈ 52.0 g based on 550 g serving size.
